ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general almond entry (FOODON_03301710); no dedicated blanched-almond sub-entry exists. Closest parent match used; blanching removes the skin but the core nut composition is equivalent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers almonds generically. No blanched-specific sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue; closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 72512008 refers to almond as a substance; no blanched-specific concept exists. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of blanched almonds is approximately 30 g (about 23 whole kernels), providing roughly 177 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Blanched almonds — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09030021 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per-100 g values are taken from this source. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 12061, Almonds, blanched) was consulted as a cross-reference and values are consistent within expected variation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 15 is reported for almonds in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Blanched almonds (skin removed) have a composition closely equivalent to whole almonds for GI purposes; the skin removal does not materially alter the fat-protein-fibre matrix that governs glucose response. GL per 30 g serving = (15 × 18.67 × 0.30) / 100 = 0.84, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (19 mg/100 g) and high potassium (659 mg/100 g) with a favourable Na:K ratio supporting blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Blanched almonds are well-suited for hypertensive individuals; a 30 g daily portion contributes approximately 6 mg sodium and 198 mg potassium, consistent with DASH dietary targets, provided salted preparations are avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 15) driven by high dietary fibre (9.9 g/100 g), protein (21.4 g/100 g), and predominantly unsaturated fat, which collectively attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 30 g portion of blanched almonds produces a glycemic load of approximately 0.8, making them a clinically appropriate snack for people with type 2 diabetes; portion control is nonetheless important given the high caloric density of 590 kcal/100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat (oleic acid ~33.6 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (~12.3 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (~3.95 g/100 g), combined with high vitamin E (25.6 mg/100 g) and absence of trans fat and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of 30 g/day of blanched almonds is associated with reductions in LDL-cholesterol and improvements in endothelial function per meta-analyses of nut intervention trials; the favourable fatty acid profile and high vitamin E content support cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin E (25.6 mg/100 g) and dominant MUFA/PUFA profile with absent trans fat support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Blanched almonds qualify under the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out for whole nuts; their vitamin E and polyphenol content (reduced but not eliminated by blanching) may modestly support antioxidant defence in rheumatoid arthritis, and unsalted preparations are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blanched almonds are whole almond kernels (Prunus dulcis) from which the brown outer skin has been removed by brief immersion in boiling water followed by cooling and mechanical peeling, leaving a smooth, ivory-white surface. The texture is firm and crunchy, and the flavour is mildly sweet and nutty, slightly less bitter than skin-on raw almonds because the tannin-rich skin has been removed. Nutritionally, blanched almonds are energy-dense at 590 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 52.5 g dominated by heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids (oleic acid ~33.6 g) and polyunsaturated fatty acids (~12.3 g), and a low saturated fat content of approximately 3.95 g. Protein is substantial at 21.4 g per 100 g, making them one of the more protein-rich plant snacks. Dietary fibre is high at 9.9 g per 100 g. Sodium is very low at 19 mg per 100 g, while potassium is high at 659 mg, supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure health. Vitamin E content is exceptionally high at approximately 25.6 mg per 100 g, providing strong antioxidant activity. The glycemic index is very low at approximately 15, making blanched almonds suitable for glycemic management. They are widely used in baking, confectionery, marzipan, almond flour, almond milk, and as a standalone snack. Blanched almonds are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and are a staple ingredient across Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, South Asian, and Western cuisines. Clinically, they are appropriate for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when consumed in moderate portions of approximately 30 g per day.
common_user_queries: Are blanched almonds good for diabetics?
What is the GI of blanched almonds?
How many calories in blanched almonds?
Are blanched almonds healthier than raw almonds?
Can I eat blanched almonds if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between blanched and raw almonds?
How much protein in blanched almonds?
How many almonds in a 30g serving?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030021, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central NDB 12061 (Almonds, blanched) with consistent results. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) publication. Fatty acid breakdown and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central where HPB did not report them, with source noted.
notes: 1. Core macros (protein_g 21.4, fat_g 52.52, carbohydrate_g 18.67) are all non-zero and well-supported; no zero-macro justification required. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; almonds are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; almonds do not contain industrially produced trans fats. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for blanched almonds. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: USDA FoodData Central reports a very small RAE value for almonds but HPB does not report it; value set to -1 pending confirmation from a single authoritative source for this specific blanched preparation. This is documented as data not available. 3. Saturated fat (3.95 g), monounsaturated fat (33.6 g), polyunsaturated fat (12.33 g), and detailed micronutrients (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12061 (Almonds, blanched) as HPB does not report these fields; this is documented as a supplementary cross-reference. 4. Blanching removes the tannin-rich skin; polyphenol content is slightly lower than skin-on raw almonds but the macronutrient and fatty acid profile is essentially equivalent. 5. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been omitted from classification as almonds are not a grain product; all other dietary tags (plant_based, low_sodium, high_protein, high_fibre, high_fat_warning) are reflected in dietary_flags and health_context. 6. No Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification applies; G6 multilingual naming compliance is satisfied as no mandatory local-language names are required for this globally generic food item.
